design_philosophy.core_loop: Accept missions (trading, bounty-hunting, cargo transport) → Engage in combat or trading → Earn currency → Upgrade ship weaponry and equipment → Access new star systems and opportunities
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Economic progression versus combat capability. Players must balance accumulating wealth through safer trading/mining activities against investing in combat upgrades needed for lucrative but dangerous missions.
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Become a self-made spacefarer entrepreneur; transition from struggling pilot in a starter ship to elite trader/mercenary with advanced weaponry across a vast galaxy. Freedom to pursue any career path (pirate, trader, bounty hunter, miner).
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Mouse-click-based space flight simulator that deliberately breaks from joystick conventions. Emphasizes economic simulation and player agency (choose any role from start in multiplayer) over narrative linearity. Single-player story is optional framing; multiplayer is the 'true' game loop. Simplified flight physics paired with momentum conservation creates accessible-but-skill-respecting combat.

difficulty.lives_system: Persistent character progression; failure in combat results in financial loss and repair costs rather than death penalty
difficulty.continues_system: Respawn at last docked location; can resume after mission failure with accumulated losses
difficulty.checkpointing: Docking at stations/planets auto-saves progress; multiplayer servers maintain persistent character records across sessions
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: Very high. Players self-regulate difficulty by choosing mission types, combat intensity, and trading routes. Early game heavily emphasizes trading/mining as low-risk wealth accumulation before investing in combat capability.

progression.upgrade_system: Ship weaponry and equipment modules purchased with accumulated credits. Better weapons and armor unlock through monetary progression, not gatekeeping. Starting ship is functional; progression is economic optimization rather than forced obsolescence.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Exploration-based. New star systems open as player travels through jump gates and trade lanes. Multiplayer mode grants access to all 48 systems from start; single-player reveals them through story missions and trading routes.
replayability_hooks: Multiplayer persistent galaxy with emergent economy and faction warfare
Multiple career paths: pirate, trader, bounty hunter, miner, mercenary
Contraband smuggling mechanics create risk/reward gameplay loops
Procedural mission generation from NPCs on stations/planets
Server-based progression allows character retention across play sessions

world_building_techniques: Faction system: four major houses (Liberty, Bretonia, Kusari, Rheinland) each representing distinct terrestrial cultures (1920s USA, Victorian UK, Shogunate Japan, Industrial Germany)
Environmental storytelling through system design: Sirius sector divided among factions with political intrigue opportunities
Historical framing: 800-year gap from Starlancer; solar system civil war led to stasis ship exodus to Sirius
NPC dialogue and bulletin boards on stations distribute mission context and lore
Persistent alien threat in single-player (mysterious force threatening Sirius sector) provides optional narrative frame

faction_indicators: Four major houses represent distinct political and cultural identities (Liberty, Bretonia, Kusari, Rheinland)
Pirate faction (descendants of Hispania) as outlaw alternative to house authority
Contraband goods tied to faction allegiances; smuggling creates faction-based conflict
danger_signals: Police patrols in faction-controlled systems respond to contraband detection
Faction-specific hostility based on player economic activity and allegiances
Pirate activity in lawless regions and outer systems
environmental_storytelling: 48 star systems with distinct economic niches (mining operations, trade stations, hideouts, derelicts)
Asteroid and debris fields create navigational hazards and hidden locations
Space highways (trade lanes) reveal economic infrastructure and faction control
Planetary settlements show cultural heritage of founding factions
